result.markdown:
--- title: "Black-Scholes option — price, Greeks, and one vectorized grid" subtitle: "The same symbolic graph prices a book and sweeps a spot ladder" --- This notebook builds a European Black-Scholes pricer as a `FormulaGraph`, prices a tiny book of a call and a put with **semantic market roles**, and then reuses *the very same graph* to sweep a 300-point spot ladder — no rewrite, no loop, just one lazy Polars plan over a wider frame. ::: {#dbbb7f0f .cell execution_count=1} ``` {.python .cell-code code-fold="true" code-summary="Imports & display helpers"} import numpy as np import polars as pl import matplotlib.pyplot as plt from datetime import date from schenberg import ( CURVES, FIXINGS, VOLS, FormulaGraph, MarketSnapshot, SchenbergDataFrameModel, With, bind, exp, log, normal_cdf, normal_pdf, sqrt, where, ) from schenberg_quarto import show_formulas, show_graph plt.rcParams.update({"figure.dpi": 110, "axes.grid": True, "grid.alpha": 0.3}) ``` ::: ## 1. Market roles → resolved columns `spot`, `risk_free_rate`, and `vol` are declared as roles. `bind(...)` resolves them from the `fixings`, `curves`, and `vol_surface` sources before the graph ever runs. ::: {#737e8268 .cell execution_count=2} ``` {.python .cell-code} RiskFree = CURVES.zero_rate("BRL_DI", as_="risk_free_rate").source("curves").for_tenor( "payment_days" ) Spot = FIXINGS.value(as_="spot").source("fixings").by(currency_pair="currency_pair") Vol = ( VOLS.implied("USD/BRL", as_="vol") .source("vol_surface") .for_expiry("expiry") .for_strike("strike") ) class OptionInput(With[Spot], With[RiskFree], With[Vol], SchenbergDataFrameModel): instrument_id: str option_type: str currency_pair: str curve: str currency: str pricing_date: date expiry: date strike: float time_to_maturity: float payment_days: int ``` ::: ## 2. The Black-Scholes graph, in headless formulas ::: {#6fd59a7c .cell execution_count=3} ``` {.python .cell-code} g = FormulaGraph("vanilla_option", input=OptionInput) @g.formula(symbol="d_1") def d1(spot, strike, risk_free_rate, vol, time_to_maturity): return (log(spot / strike) + (risk_free_rate + 0.5 * vol**2) * time_to_maturity) / ( vol * sqrt(time_to_maturity) ) @g.formula(symbol="d_2") def d2(d1, vol, time_to_maturity): return d1 - vol * sqrt(time_to_maturity) @g.formula(symbol="C") def call_value(spot, strike, risk_free_rate, time_to_maturity, d1, d2): return spot * normal_cdf(d1) - strike * exp(-risk_free_rate * time_to_maturity) * normal_cdf(d2) @g.formula(symbol="P") def put_value(spot, strike, risk_free_rate, time_to_maturity, d1, d2): return strike * exp(-risk_free_rate * time_to_maturity) * normal_cdf(-d2) - spot * normal_cdf( -d1 ) @g.formula(symbol="V") def value(option_type, call_value, put_value): return where(option_type == "call", call_value, put_value) @g.formula(symbol="\\Delta") def delta(option_type, d1): return where(option_type == "call", normal_cdf(d1), normal_cdf(d1) - 1.0) @g.formula(symbol="\\Gamma") def gamma(spot, vol, time_to_maturity, d1): return normal_pdf(d1) / (spot * vol * sqrt(time_to_maturity)) @g.formula(symbol="\\nu") def vega(spot, time_to_maturity, d1): return spot * normal_pdf(d1) * sqrt(time_to_maturity) g.returns( "output", instrument_id="instrument_id", option_type="option_type", value="value", delta="delta", gamma="gamma", vega="vega", ) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=3} ``` ``` ::: ::: The math, derived from the IR: ``` {.python .cell-code} show_formulas(g) ``` **Formulas** $$ d_1 = \frac{\ln\left(\frac{\mathrm{spot}}{\mathrm{strike}}\right) + \left(\mathrm{risk\_free\_rate} + 0.5 \cdot \mathrm{vol}^{2}\right) \cdot \mathrm{time\_to\_maturity}}{\mathrm{vol} \cdot \sqrt{\mathrm{time\_to\_maturity}}} $$ $$ d_2 = \mathrm{d1} - \mathrm{vol} \cdot \sqrt{\mathrm{time\_to\_maturity}} $$ $$ C = \mathrm{spot} \cdot \Phi\left(\mathrm{d1}\right) - \mathrm{strike} \cdot e^{-\mathrm{risk\_free\_rate} \cdot \mathrm{time\_to\_maturity}} \cdot \Phi\left(\mathrm{d2}\right) $$ $$ P = \mathrm{strike} \cdot e^{-\mathrm{risk\_free\_rate} \cdot \mathrm{time\_to\_maturity}} \cdot \Phi\left(-\mathrm{d2}\right) - \mathrm{spot} \cdot \Phi\left(-\mathrm{d1}\right) $$ $$ V = \begin{cases} \mathrm{call\_value} & \mathrm{option\_type} = call \\ \mathrm{put\_value} & \text{else} \end{cases} $$ $$ \Delta = \begin{cases} \Phi\left(\mathrm{d1}\right) & \mathrm{option\_type} = call \\ \Phi\left(\mathrm{d1}\right) - 1.0 & \text{else} \end{cases} $$ $$ \Gamma = \frac{\phi\left(\mathrm{d1}\right)}{\mathrm{spot} \cdot \mathrm{vol} \cdot \sqrt{\mathrm{time\_to\_maturity}}} $$ $$ \nu = \mathrm{spot} \cdot \phi\left(\mathrm{d1}\right) \cdot \sqrt{\mathrm{time\_to\_maturity}} $$ ``` {.python .cell-code} show_graph(g) ``` ```{mermaid} flowchart LR spot --> d1 strike --> d1 risk_free_rate --> d1 vol --> d1 time_to_maturity --> d1 d1 --> d2 vol --> d2 time_to_maturity --> d2 spot --> call_value d1 --> call_value strike --> call_value risk_free_rate --> call_value time_to_maturity --> call_value d2 --> call_value strike --> put_value risk_free_rate --> put_value time_to_maturity --> put_value d2 --> put_value spot --> put_value d1 --> put_value option_type --> value call_value --> value put_value --> value option_type --> delta d1 --> delta d1 --> gamma spot --> gamma vol --> gamma time_to_maturity --> gamma spot --> vega d1 --> vega time_to_maturity --> vega ``` ## 3. Price a book ::: {#fee47ba9 .cell execution_count=6} ``` {.python .cell-code} trades = pl.DataFrame( { "instrument_id": ["OPT-CALL-1", "OPT-PUT-1"], "option_type": ["call", "put"], "currency_pair": ["USD/BRL", "USD/BRL"], "curve": ["BRL_DI", "BRL_DI"], "currency": ["BRL", "BRL"], "pricing_date": [date(2026, 6, 6), date(2026, 6, 6)], "expiry": [date(2027, 6, 6), date(2027, 6, 6)], "strike": [100.0, 100.0], "time_to_maturity": [1.0, 1.0], "payment_days": [252, 252], } ).lazy() market = ( MarketSnapshot.at(date(2026, 6, 6)) .source( "fixings", pl.DataFrame({"currency_pair": ["USD/BRL"], "value": [100.0]}), unique_by=("currency_pair",), ) .source( "curves", pl.DataFrame({"curve": ["BRL_DI"], "tenor_days": [252], "zero_rate": [0.05]}), unique_by=("curve", "tenor_days"), ) .source( "vol_surface", pl.DataFrame( { "currency_pair": ["USD/BRL"], "expiry": [date(2027, 6, 6)], "strike": [100.0], "implied_vol": [0.20], } ), unique_by=("currency_pair", "expiry", "strike"), ) .build() ) g.plan(bind(trades, market, OptionInput), view="output").collect() ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=6} ```{=html}
shape: (2, 17)

0.20.05100.0"OPT-PUT-1""put""USD/BRL""BRL_DI""BRL"2026-06-062027-06-06100.01.02525.573518-0.3631690.01876237.524035
``` ::: ::: ATM, S=K=100, r=5%, σ=20%, T=1: the call is worth ≈ 10.45 and the put ≈ 5.57, and call − put = S − K·e^(−rT) is put-call parity — a good first sanity check. ## 4. Reuse the graph over a spot ladder The graph is just a pure function of its columns. If we hand it a frame that *already* has `spot`, `strike`, … (no `bind` needed), it sweeps the whole ladder in one shot. We ask only for the `value`/`delta`/`gamma`/`vega` terms via an explicit `outputs=` mapping. ::: {#09da9ac6 .cell execution_count=7} ``` {.python .cell-code} S = np.linspace(60.0, 140.0, 300) grid = pl.DataFrame( { "spot": S, "strike": np.full_like(S, 100.0), "risk_free_rate": np.full_like(S, 0.05), "vol": np.full_like(S, 0.20), "time_to_maturity": np.full_like(S, 1.0), "option_type": ["call"] * S.size, } ).lazy() swept = g.plan( grid, outputs={"value": "value", "delta": "delta", "gamma": "gamma", "vega": "vega"}, ).collect() fig, ax = plt.subplots(2, 2, figsize=(9, 6), constrained_layout=True) ax[0, 0].plot(S, swept["value"], color="#2563eb"); ax[0, 0].set_title("Call value") ax[0, 1].plot(S, swept["delta"], color="#16a34a"); ax[0, 1].set_title("Delta") ax[1, 0].plot(S, swept["gamma"], color="#db2777"); ax[1, 0].set_title("Gamma") ax[1, 1].plot(S, swept["vega"], color="#d97706"); ax[1, 1].set_title("Vega") for a in ax.flat: a.axvline(100.0, color="0.6", ls="--", lw=1) a.set_xlabel("spot") fig.suptitle("One graph, 300 spots — value and Greeks across the ladder") plt.show() ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display} ![](02_vanilla_option_files/figure-html/cell-8-output-1.png){} ::: ::: These Greeks are *closed-form* expressions in the same graph. The next notebook shows how Schenberg gets the **second-order** Greeks (vanna, volga) for free — by differentiating the price formula's IR with JAX, no algebra by hand.
